text: The surgeon performs a posterior dissection of the pulmonary artery during a left upper lobectomy for lung cancer. The level XI lymph nodes are visible along the pulmonary artery in the interlobar plane, with the posterior fissure underlying these nodes. The surgeon carefully dissects this area to ensure the stapler can slide through without encountering undivided parenchyma and lymph nodes. The glistening surface of the pulmonary artery is exposed, and the first branch to the left upper lobe is identified, marked with a cigar sponge.

The surgeon uses a zero-degree scope to dissect the pulmonary artery, removing level ten and aortopulmonary lymph nodes to clear lymphatic and soft tissue around the artery. The dissection reveals the branches of the pulmonary artery, providing a clear view of the anatomy.

The surgeon carefully dissects the level eleven lymph node from the bronchus to the lower lobe using a bipolar instrument. The pulmonary artery is exposed in the fissure, despite the incomplete division of the fissure. The inferior vein is visible, with the superior vein located cephalad to the working area. The surgeon identifies the junction of the left upper lobe and left lower lobe bronchi in the hilum from an anterior approach.

The surgeon cauterizes bronchial arteries to prevent bleeding, then continues dissection to reveal the junction between the upper and lower lobe bronchi from an anterior approach. A stapler is introduced through the number three port to divide the thin anterior fissure, aiming for the space between the upper and lower lobe bronchi. The anatomy becomes clearer as the dissection progresses, with the pulmonary artery visible behind the level eleven lymph node.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the lymph node, revealing the pulmonary artery. A sponge is used to maintain a dry field. The surgeon positions the stapler on the PA plane and fires it, opening the space and exposing approximately two centimeters of tissue, facilitating further dissection of the pulmonary artery.

The surgeon places the anvil on top of the pulmonary artery and divides the parenchyma, opening up the space. The lymph node is removed from the area adjacent to the pulmonary artery. The surgeon carefully pulls the parenchyma into the stapler, working on the fissure, which is notably thick. The pulmonary artery is exposed, and the surgeon decides to take the lingular branch using a vascular stapler with a curved tip through the anterior stapling port.

The surgeon carefully removes a cigar sponge from the staple line on the pulmonary artery, ensuring no staples catch on the sponge. A staple fire is used to open up the fissure through port number three, reducing the veil of parenchyma for better visibility. Blue loads are used for the parenchyma, while green loads are reserved for bronchi, depending on the presence of fibrosis or other parenchymal disease.

The surgeon positions the stapler anvil on the pulmonary artery and pulls the parenchyma into the stapler to fire, completing the fissure between the upper and lower lobes. A suction irrigator is used to maintain a clear field, and a curved tip stapler with a blue load is employed to finalize the dissection. The superior segmental branch to the lower lobe is visible, and the surgeon plans to take branches along the pulmonary artery from distal to proximal.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide a small PA branch under tension, opting for energy division over stapling. The retraction arm is adjusted to continue dissection towards the proximal pulmonary artery, taking branches until visibility decreases. The surgeon then transitions to the anterior hilum, preparing to take the vein by opening the pleura and lymphatic tissue surrounding it.

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to carefully navigate around the vessels, ensuring the vein is identified and not the pulmonary artery. The grasper is positioned against the bronchus, with its tip visible in the areolar tissue to avoid damaging vascular structures. A vessel loop is placed around the vein, and a tip-up vascular load stapler is prepared through the posterior port for further dissection.

The surgeon approaches the case from anterior to posterior, retracting the lung to access the vein and pulmonary artery branches. A small injury to the chest wall causes bleeding, which is quickly managed. The surgeon continues dissection on the pulmonary artery, removing lymph nodes between the bronchus and artery, ensuring a clear path for the stapler to pass from anterior to posterior.

The surgeon uses a long bipolar grasper to dissect around the bronchus and pulmonary artery, which is clearly visible cephalad to the bronchus. The crotch between the ongoing pulmonary artery and its branches is identified, allowing for further dissection around the artery branches. A tip-up grasper is used to push against the bronchus, ensuring the tip is visible and maneuvering carefully to avoid catching unseen structures. A vessel loop is passed around the branches, followed by the use of a tip-up vascular stapler to take these branches.

The surgeon uses a curved tip vascular stapler to transect the last pulmonary artery branch, ensuring all branches are addressed by flipping the parenchyma anteriorly for confirmation. The bronchus is then compressed and transected using a green load stapler, completing the lobectomy. The remaining lymph nodes in the aorta pulmonary window are removed before releasing the capnothorax.
